schools.summary: Gill: this was thirty-eight years after the foregoing events, not the congregation that came out of Egypt, whose carcasses had fallen in the wilderness; Miriam, the prophetess, died and was buried at Kadesh.

themes: the people abode in Kadesh; and Miriam died there, and was buried there
a new generation, the old being dead
the leaders mortal, God's work going on

schools.summary: Gill: this was ordered for the trial of the new generation, to see whether they would behave better than their fathers in a like case the first year out of Egypt.
Keil & Delitzsch: in the arid desert the congregation was in want of water and gathered themselves against Moses and Aaron.

themes: there was no water for the congregation: and they gathered themselves together against Moses
the want of water a trial
the fathers' sin of murmuring repeated

schools.summary: Gill: they contended in a wrangling, litigious manner, showing no reverence for his office or the favours received — and this at a time when they should have condoled him on the loss of Miriam.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people chode with Moses, wishing they had died when their brethren died before the LORD.

themes: the people chode with Moses... Would God that we had died when our brethren died before the LORD
reproaching their leader in his grief
ingratitude forgetting all past mercy

schools.summary: Gill: they were now come by various marches and stations into the wilderness of Zin, and accused their leaders of bringing 'the congregation of the LORD' to die there.

themes: why have ye brought up the congregation of the LORD into this wilderness... should die there?
blaming the instrument, not seeing God's hand
forgetting it was the cloud that led them

schools.summary: Gill: they represented the matter as if forced out of Egypt against their wills, forgetting the bondage and bitter slavery they had cried out under, and reviled the place as barren — no seed, figs, vines, or pomegranates, nor water.

themes: wherefore have ye made us to come up out of Egypt, to bring us in unto this evil place?
the heart still hankering after Egypt
despising the redemption past and the land to come

schools.summary: Gill: they went like fugitives, fleeing through fear lest the people stone them, to the door of the tabernacle where the Lord had promised to meet Moses. Calvin: they fled in fear, yet to seek the LORD.

themes: Moses and Aaron went... unto the door of the tabernacle... and the glory of the LORD appeared
prayer their refuge in danger
the glory the pledge God had not forsaken them

schools.summary: Gill: God spake out of the token of his glory, perhaps the cloud with an uncommon brightness.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD now relieved the want of water, directing Moses what to do.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses
mercy for murmuring
the patience of God answering sin with kindness

schools.summary: Gill: the rod of miracles, the rod of Moses with which he had done wonders in Egypt and at Horeb, where he had smitten the rock the first year.
Keil & Delitzsch: take the rod, gather the assembly, and speak to the rock before their eyes, and it shall give forth water.

themes: speak ye unto the rock before their eyes; and it shall give forth his water
the word, not the blow, commanded
God's power shown at his bare word

schools.summary: Gill: the rod was laid up somewhere in the sanctuary, as was Aaron's rod.
Wesley: 'from before the LORD' is out of the tabernacle.

themes: Moses took the rod from before the LORD, as he commanded him
beginning in obedience
long faithfulness no guard against one fall

schools.summary: Gill: they gathered not the elders only but as many of the congregation as could be assembled, before the rock to which they were directed.
Keil & Delitzsch: Moses said, 'Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?'

themes: Hear now, ye rebels; must we fetch you water out of this rock?
spoken in passion and self-assertion
the meek man unadvised in his lips (Ps. 106:33)

schools.summary: Gill: at first it brought but a few drops, so he smote again and it flowed plentifully; he relays the Targum's tradition of blood at the first stroke and water at the second, answering the blood and water from Christ's pierced side (John 19:34); the rock was Christ (1 Cor. 10:4).

themes: with his rod he smote the rock twice: and the water came out abundantly
that Rock was Christ, the smitten Saviour (1 Cor. 10:4)
the double stroke marring the type (John 19:34)

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD spake out of the cloud where his glory still appeared.
Clarke: God bade Moses speak to the rock, but he smote it, and twice, and said 'must we fetch water,' arrogating to himself and Aaron what was God's, and in anger — the offence of unbelief.

themes: Because ye believed me not, to sanctify me... ye shall not bring this congregation into the land
the root of the sin unbelief and self-assertion
God hallowed in them that come near

schools.summary: Gill: Meribah means 'strife,' sometimes called Meribah-Kadesh to distinguish it from the earlier strife at Horeb.
Keil & Delitzsch: the name from the children of Israel striving with the LORD, and he was sanctified in them.

themes: This is the water of Meribah; because the children of Israel strove with the LORD
strife against God himself
he was sanctified, in mercy and in holiness

schools.summary: Gill: Edom was sometimes governed by kings, sometimes by dukes; the king here, thirty-nine years after the Exodus, Usher takes to be Hadar; Moses styled Israel 'thy brother,' Edom being descended from Esau.

themes: Moses sent messengers... unto the king of Edom, Thus saith thy brother Israel
a peaceable embassy appealing to kinship
seeking passage by entreaty, not the sword

schools.summary: Gill: Jacob and his twelve sons with their children went down into Egypt.
Keil & Delitzsch: they dwelt in Egypt a long time, and the Egyptians vexed them and their fathers.

themes: How our fathers went down into Egypt... and the Egyptians vexed us
the recital of bondage
a courteous appeal grounded in shared history

schools.summary: Gill: they cried to the LORD by reason of their bondage and to be delivered from it.
Ellicott: the 'angel' is the Angel of the LORD who went before them (Exod. 14:19); 'hath brought us forth' is better 'brought us forth.'

themes: when we cried unto the LORD, he heard... and sent an angel, and... brought us forth
confessing God's deliverance before the heathen
standing at the border, asking leave

schools.summary: Gill: this was the nearest and shortest way to Canaan from where they were.
Clarke: the first mention of 'the king's highway,' a public road made by the king's authority at the expense of the state.

themes: Let us pass... we will not pass through the fields... we will go by the king's high way
a studiously inoffensive request
leaving Edom no honest ground for refusal

schools.summary: Gill: the king of Edom replied to Israel by the messengers — 'thou shalt not pass.'
Wesley: an act of policy, to secure themselves from so numerous a host, refusing passage through their country.

themes: Thou shalt not pass by me, lest I come out against thee with the sword
meeting entreaty with threat
the unbrotherliness arraigned by the prophets (Obad. 10)

schools.summary: Gill: the messengers made answer to the king of Edom.
Keil & Delitzsch: they offered to go by the highway, pay for any water, and only pass through on foot, doing nothing else.

themes: We will go by the high way: and if... we drink of thy water, then I will pay for it
meekness answering churlishness
restraint under God's command

schools.summary: Gill: this was an absolute and peremptory denial.
Keil & Delitzsch: to give emphasis to his refusal, Edom came out against Israel with much people and a strong hand.

themes: he said, Thou shalt not go through. And Edom came out against him with much people
the refusal backed by an army
ready to fight a kinsman over a harmless passage

schools.summary: Gill: the refusal stood notwithstanding their near relation and Israel's fair promises.
Keil & Delitzsch: Israel turned away from him and journeyed on.

themes: Edom refused to give Israel passage... wherefore Israel turned away from him
declining to force the passage
obedient yielding, suffering wrong rather than break God's command

schools.summary: Gill: the 'whole congregation' went from Kadesh complete, not one lost by Edom's coming out against them.
Henry: the chapter began with the funeral of Miriam and ends with that of Aaron.

themes: the children of Israel, even the whole congregation, journeyed from Kadesh, and came unto mount Hor
the chapter framed by two funerals
the guides falling while the people go on

schools.summary: Gill: they were at the foot of the mountain, in the valley adjoining.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD spake at Mount Hor, by the coast of the land of Edom.

themes: the LORD spake unto Moses and Aaron in mount Hor
announcing Aaron's death beforehand
God ordering the passing of his servant

schools.summary: Gill: 'gathered unto his people' is a periphrasis for death, used of good and bad alike.
Ellicott: the phrase does not refer to the place of burial (Gen. 25:8).
Wesley: the rebellion at Meribah was one reason, though not the only one, that God would not have Aaron enter.

themes: Aaron shall be gathered unto his people... because ye rebelled... at the water of Meribah
justice and mercy joined
received home though shut out of earthly Canaan (Gen. 25:8)

schools.summary: Gill: Eleazar was Aaron's eldest son, who was to succeed him in the priesthood, and did. Calvin: the successor was designated while Aaron lived, that the perpetuity of the priesthood be secured and the people not take the election on themselves, the office depending on the will of God.

themes: Take Aaron and Eleazar his son, and bring them up unto mount Hor
the successor designated while the father lived
God providing for the unbroken ministry

schools.summary: Gill: the priestly garments, as the Targum says.
Clarke: the stripping was in effect depriving Aaron of his office, and the clothing of Eleazar a transfer of it — an investiture, as removing one was a divestiture.

themes: strip Aaron of his garments, and put them upon Eleazar his son
the visible passing of the priesthood
the mortal office handed on (Heb. 7:23)

schools.summary: Gill: how cutting and afflicting it must have been to Moses to part with a brother so dear, yet being the Lord's will he submitted and faithfully obeyed.
Ellicott: they went up into Mount Hor in the sight of all the congregation, some rendering 'to the summit.'

themes: Moses did as the LORD commanded... in the sight of all the congregation
leading his own brother up to die
a triumph of submission to God's will

schools.summary: Gill: the same hands that clothed Aaron at first now stripped him, both at God's command; the stripping a figure of the disannulling of his priesthood when the Messiah, a priest after another order, should come; Aaron died on the top of the mount (Heb. 7:24).

themes: Moses stripped Aaron of his garments... and Aaron died there in the top of the mount
the supersession of the Aaronic order
the need of the one Priest who dies no more (Heb. 7:24)

schools.summary: Gill: they did not see his body but perceived it by Moses' report and the circumstances — Eleazar coming down with Aaron's garments on, and the tokens of mourning; the Targums add they saw Moses and Eleazar come down with garments rent and ashes on their heads; the whole house of Israel mourned thirty days.

themes: when all the congregation saw that Aaron was dead, they mourned for Aaron thirty days
the long and general grief honouring the high priest
the death of a faithful minister a public sorrow
